text.title: Status Codes and HATEOAS
text.summary: Correct status code selection for each operation outcome and optional HATEOAS link pattern.

rule.statement: Use 400 for client input that fails syntactic or semantic validation; 422 for input that is structurally valid but violates business rules.
rule.rationale: The distinction matters for client error handling: 400 means "fix the request format"; 422 means "fix the business logic inputs".

rule.statement: Use 401 when authentication credentials are missing or invalid; 403 when credentials are valid but permission is denied.
rule.rationale: Conflating 401 and 403 confuses clients about whether re-authenticating will help.

rule.statement: Use 409 for state conflicts (duplicate resource, optimistic lock failure); not for validation errors.
rule.rationale: 409 signals a conflict with current server state — the client may retry after resolving the conflict, unlike a validation error.

rule.statement: Never return 200 with an error body. Use the appropriate 4xx or 5xx code.
rule.rationale: HTTP clients and monitoring tools inspect status codes, not body content, to detect errors. 200 with error body defeats all standard tooling.

rule.statement: Include a _links object with self, related resources, and available actions when clients need to discover next steps dynamically.
rule.rationale: HATEOAS decouples clients from URL structure — they follow links rather than constructing URLs, reducing breakage on API refactors.
